prompt: Summarize GPU profiling in one short sentence.
Explain TTFT in a single sentence for an engineer who is new to serving latency.
Write two compact bullet points about why prefix caching matters for repeated prompts in LLM serving.
Describe, in plain language, the difference between prefill and generation for a text-generation request handled by an OpenAI-compatible inference server.
Give a concise explanation of why queueing delay, first-token latency, and end-to-end latency are related but not identical measurements when benchmarking or profiling an inference service.
List three practical reasons an engineer might collect both client-side latency numbers and kernel-level GPU traces when diagnosing poor throughput or high p99 latency in a production LLM endpoint.

In two or three sentences, explain how a vLLM-compatible server might expose useful Prometheus metrics, including request concurrency and KV cache usage, and why those metrics help distinguish scheduler pressure from raw model compute time.

Write a compact paragraph about how a traffic replay tool can approximate real user experience while still failing to reveal true server-side queue start, prefill start, and decode boundary timings unless the serving engine emits per-request lifecycle events.

Provide a short engineering note about why token counts taken from model response usage fields are usually more trustworthy than rough character-length heuristics when a profiler wants to classify workloads, estimate disaggregation break-even points, or reason about prefill cost versus generation cost.

Draft a crisp internal note for a performance team: explain that a live smoke test against a real model should verify that throughput is plausible, TTFT is non-zero, generation latency is non-zero, prompt token counts come from response usage when available, placeholder sections are explicitly marked unavailable, and no report row prints confident zeroes that would imply server-truth where only proxies or missing data exist.
